does: This paper conducts a systematic safety audit of eight state-of-the-art open-source large language models using interpretability-based jailbreaking techniques. The authors leverage two interpretability approaches—Universal Steering and Representation Engineering—to identify and exploit vulnerabilities in model internals by discovering optimal activation-steering coefficients that elicit unsafe behaviors. They evaluate these attacks on curated sets of harmful queries using an LLM-based judging protocol, revealing substantial variation in model robustness. Key findings show Llama-3 models are highly vulnerable with jailbreak success rates up to 91% under Universal Steering, while GPT-oss-120B demonstrates robust resistance to both attack methods. The work includes an adaptive two-stage grid search algorithm designed to systematically locate effective steering parameters for unsafe behavioral concepts across different model architectures and scales.
connects: This work builds on emerging interpretability-driven safety research that moves beyond black-box adversarial testing toward mechanistic understanding of model vulnerabilities. It extends prior work on activation steering and representation engineering—techniques that manipulate internal model representations to control behavior—into the adversarial domain. The paper responds to growing concerns about interpretability-based jailbreaks demonstrated in recent research on mechanistic vulnerabilities in language models. It connects to the broader literature on safety auditing and red-teaming of LLMs, particularly studies examining how model internals encode harmful capabilities. The systematic comparison across multiple model families and scales builds on comparative vulnerability studies while introducing interpretability as a lens for understanding why certain architectures prove more robust than others.
enables: This work establishes interpretability-based jailbreaking as a practical auditing tool for AI safety teams, moving beyond proof-of-concept toward systematic vulnerability assessment. It enables future research into why certain architectures like GPT-oss-120B resist interpretation-guided attacks while others like Llama-3 remain vulnerable, potentially informing better safety training methods. The identified vulnerabilities suggest future work on interpretability-aware alignment techniques that defend against activation steering at the mechanistic level. The comparative analysis across model families and scales creates a foundation for understanding how architectural choices, training procedures, and scale affect susceptibility to interpretability-based attacks. This could drive development of more robust safety mechanisms and inform best practices for pre-deployment safety auditing of frontier models.
